column1: #Summary1=(human1) Three speakers express surprise in learning the history of recognition of black holes – not first postulated in 1915 by Einstein – rather citing definitions of black holes as first posited in 1783 by geologist John Mitchell in a letter, and in 1796 by mathematician Pierre-Simon Laplace in a section of his book which was later removed due to controversy, persisting today.

#Summary2=(claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929; postedited) On the show, Kevin, Torenm and a third speaker discuss black holes, tracing their first postulation to 1783 by John Mitchell, examining how they form through processes like supernova collapse and neutron star mergers, and exploring concepts like event horizons and different types of black holes.

#Summary3=(gpt4o; postedited) In this podcast, Kevin, Toren, and a third host discuss the historical origins of black hole theories dating back to John Mitchell in 1783 and Pierre-Simon Laplace in 1796, delve into the processes that form black holes, and explore the controversies and mysteries surrounding black hole science and terminology.

#Summary4=(Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct) The concept of black holes was first proposed in 1783 by geologist John Mitchell and later promoted by mathematician Pierre-Simon Laplace in his book Exposition du système du monde.

#Summary5=(Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) The concept of black holes was first postulated in 1783 by geologist John Mitchell, long before Einstein's work, and involves a body so massive that not even light can escape its gravity, with formation processes including star collapse, neutron star mergers, and potential formation from the Big Bang.
#Text=When did they first postulate black holes ?
#Text=When did they first postulate black holes ?
#Text=My actual original guess would have been Einstein .
#Text=Because was n't it -- is n't it relativistic that something ?
#Text=I 'm just going to say -- I 'm just going to say the 40s .
#Text=Well , so 1915 was when Einstein first started talking about it .
#Text=But the first positing of the existence of black holes actually dates back to 1783 .
#Text=The idea of a body so massive that even light could not escape was first put forward by geologist John Mitchell in a letter written to Henry Cavendish in 1783 to the Royal Society .

#Text=And I quote , “ if the semi-diameter of a sphere of the same density as the sun were to exceed or to exceed that of the sun , and the proportion of 500 to 1 , a body falling from an infinite height towards it would have acquired surface greater velocity than that of light , and consequently , supposing light to be attracted by the same force in proportion to its inertia with other bodies , all light emitted from such a body would be made to return towards it by its own gravity ” .
#Text=So he did the gravity math and said , according to this , it pulls down stronger than -- than the speed of light .
#Text=And in fact , in 1796 , a mathematician , Pierre - Simon Laplace , promoted the same idea in both the first and second editions of his book Exposition du système du monde .
#Text=Well , we 'll have to get copies of that .
#Text=And -- but it was removed from later editions .
#Text=It only exists in the first and second edition .
#Text=Oh , well , that 's going to be hard to find , then .
#Text=I was -- I was pretty surprised myself when I found that one out .
#Text=The densest , most massive singular objects in the universe formed in one of three main processes .
#Text=Locked in a binary relationship , spiral together and merge .
#Text=That 's the other one I was going to go with .
#Text=I was -- I was going to -- when worlds collide , was what I was going to say .
#Text=And then number three postulated by our own Lego Stephen Hawking , that trillions of black holes were formed in the Big Bang .
#Text=Trillions , so -- some of which still exist today .
#Text=I think there 's a lot of black hole controversy .

#Text=I think that when in reading up on this , I think that 's what I found to be the most interesting thing , is that pretty much every single theory about black holes seem to come with controversy .
#Text=It 's an enigma wrapped in a mystery , wrapped in --
#Text=It 's really the edge of -- of our knowledge .
#Text=Would you say it 's the event horizon of our knowledge ?
#Text=This is kind of the event horizon .
#Text=Well , I think that it 's pretty close to --
#Text=We are throwing around some black hole terminology .
#Text=Why do n't we talk about what those are actually ?
#Text=That 's where that 's basically where at the point at which you can not escape a black --
#Text=The point at which the pull of gravity would exceed that of -- would even pull in light .
#Text=So even light , the fastest thing in the universe , would not be able to escape its pull .
#Text=Yes , but are n't there three kinds of black holes , Kevin ?
#Text=You had only two on your list .
#Text=Toren already showed me up once today .
#Text=Uh , because I 'd read up about the the Schwarzschild --
#Text=The Schwarzschild black hole , which is a non-rotating black hole .
#Text=And , and I found , I found out about the Kerr black hole .
